questions.question: In a stacking ensemble, why must the Level 0 (base model) predictions be generated using cross-validation rather than simply training the base models on the full training set?
options: Cross-validation is faster than training on the full dataset for base models.

If base models are trained on the full training set and their predictions become features for the meta-learner, those predictions have 'seen' the training labels during training — causing target leakage. Cross-validation ensures each training sample's Level 0 prediction was generated by a model that never saw that sample during training.
Cross-validation prevents base models from overfitting to training data.
The meta-learner requires cross-validated predictions to compute confidence intervals.
questions.answer: If base models are trained on the full training set and their predictions become features for the meta-learner, those predictions have 'seen' the training labels during training — causing target leakage. Cross-validation ensures each training sample's Level 0 prediction was generated by a model that never saw that sample during training.
questions.explanation: Without CV in stacking: base models memorize training labels → their predictions are near-perfect on training set → meta-learner learns to trust them → massive overfitting. With 5-fold CV: each sample's base model prediction comes from a model trained on the other 4 folds only.

questions.question: Why does soft voting (averaging predicted probabilities) typically outperform hard voting (majority class vote) in classification ensembles?
options: Soft voting is faster because it averages numbers rather than counting votes.

Hard voting treats a 51% confident prediction and a 99% confident prediction equally (both count as one vote). Soft voting weights the contribution of each model by its confidence — a model that says 99% vs 51% has more influence in the final decision, resulting in better-calibrated and more accurate ensemble predictions.
Soft voting works with both classifiers and regressors; hard voting only works for classifiers.
Soft voting averages the model weights rather than the predictions.
questions.answer: Hard voting treats a 51% confident prediction and a 99% confident prediction equally (both count as one vote). Soft voting weights the contribution of each model by its confidence — a model that says 99% vs 51% has more influence in the final decision, resulting in better-calibrated and more accurate ensemble predictions.
questions.explanation: Two models predict class A at 99% confidence. One model predicts class B at 51% confidence. Hard voting: 2-1 for A. Soft voting averages: A=(0.99+0.99+0.49)/3=82%, B=18% → decisive win for A. Hard voting could tie if the third model crossed 50%.

questions.question: Why does the Out-Of-Bag (OOB) score in Random Forest provide a useful free validation estimate?
options: OOB samples are randomly selected and independently validated by multiple trees.

Each Random Forest tree is trained on a bootstrap sample (~63% of data). The remaining ~37% of samples (OOB samples) are never seen by that specific tree during training — they can be used as a validation set for that tree. Aggregating OOB predictions across all trees gives an unbiased estimate of generalization performance without needing a separate validation split.
OOB validation uses a different loss function than the training objective.
OOB estimates are only valid when max_depth is set to None.
questions.answer: Each Random Forest tree is trained on a bootstrap sample (~63% of data). The remaining ~37% of samples (OOB samples) are never seen by that specific tree during training — they can be used as a validation set for that tree. Aggregating OOB predictions across all trees gives an unbiased estimate of generalization performance without needing a separate validation split.
questions.explanation: OOB score is essentially 'free' cross-validation — it uses no extra compute beyond what's required to train the forest. For large datasets where 5-fold CV is expensive, OOB score provides a quick generalization estimate.
